item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that the image is CC BY-SA 3.0 and thus perfectly compliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They are. Ever hear of protocol 12? It's basically "in case of emergency, find other people for D-class" for certain things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder how they're gonna test the other stuff like the monkey bars, I mean I don't think the foundation is even cruel enough to use actual children for testing

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It would be cool if someone could draw these things in normal form so I can imagine what it looks like for kids. I know there's a picture but it's not doing much for me since it's on one side.

Also I have a question. Is it sometimes different for some kids? Would a kid that has a fear of tight spaces feel really tight in some of the spaces in the playground and stuff like that?

Really nice SCP and I like the idea of a kid hanging on to a bridge while falling off it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This seemed like something that would be safe for anybody to use, although frightening for children. The test log shattered that illusion rather thoroughly though.

Also, the visual of researchers and D-Class testing the equipment to see how safe it is was a rather entertaining thought.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two things.

1. Dammit. As an adult, the thing sound fun! But it only has effect on kids, so…

2. This is one of few times I actually feel sorry for a D-class. It's unlikely that he was from death row, and the Foundation probaly exclude him from monthly termination (since I'm pretty sure that a child-like D-class who's also smart and obedience enough to follow their instruction isn't easy to come by).

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the implications of a D-class with that mental capacity is actually more chilling to me than the scp itself. always is when a dclass has the mind of a child

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Image credit time: this photo is taken from Wikimedia Commons, http://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Madison_District_Play_structure_view_2.JPG and it is therefore licensed under Creative Commons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loving this. Good job on the addendum, it really works for me. It just reminds me so much of chilhood games, the fantasy worlds children built… with a sinister twist at the end. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sure, there would be a lot more added to this in the future; however, I think this works best as a piece of writing as it is, leaving all the further details and questions to the reader's imagination. Think of this, if you like, as the document as the Foundation is only just starting to investigate, a snapshot around the beginning of this SCP's journey through Foundation custody.

Still, thanks for the praise!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The writing is good and it's entertaining to read, but the Foundation wouldn't suspend testing after one D-Class dies. There's plenty of questions left to be answered here, and I want to see more logs and more tests.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you, wishun! Interesting you get a greater emotional reaction than with a child. I had initially considered a child test subject, but I couldn't bring myself to write it - plus, I didn't think that the Foundation would risk children.

I entirely agree with you that, were this real, the Foundation would mention early on that there may be actual space warping effects. It makes no sense for them not to in-universe, but as you say, I can't think how I could write it with the same emotional impact to have the twist earlier on. Chalk it up to one of the failings of the format - logically, the most important information would be very early on in the file, but narratively, it works better to have it at the end. In my defence, at least it's not something that should be in the containment procedures.

As for author insight, I present the inspiration below. Be warned: it's probably not as good as your interpretation.

Spoilers
Author inspiration

So I got the idea for this a good few months ago now, when I was out walking with a friend of mine late in the evening, as we often did. We came across a playground that was completely deserted (no doubt due to the lateness of the hour). To my surprise, he excitedly ran over to it and entreated me to join him. I was surprised because he'd never shown the slightest bit of interest in such play equipment before; when I put that to him, he explained that he loves playground equipment, but absolutely hates children.

He said that if he could, he would make a playground that would be fun for him, but children would stay away from it. I queried how he would do such a thing, and he laughed, and suggested magic. And that perhaps if the children were still stupid enough to play on it anyway, they would die in horrible ways (he really doesn't like children). It was then we noticed a distressingly pungent smell underneath one of the little hut bits where there were small shelf-like things designed for sitting on, and we hypothesised that someone had urinated there for precisely the reason of keeping children away.

As for actual interpretation of this SCP, I was in two minds about how I think it originates. One notion is that a paedophobic wizard such as my friend wished to be put a spell on a playground he liked, using a series of esoteric biochemicals whose polypeptide chains form magical symbols and his urine as a binder. The other is that it is the home ground of some anomalous animal which likes the comfort and convenience of an urban playground but not all those noisy little humans, and it has, indeed, marked its territory.

The final thing to note while we're in spoiler territory, is that the playground hasn't been in use for a while, even by who- or whatever made it how it is. Why would that be…? And why would the thing pick such an uninteresting playground? Maybe… it was just a pilot test?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is very well-written!
I had far more of an emotional reaction to the D-class than I would have with "just another" child test subject.
+1

One nitpick though. From the description by itself, one would think that the playground just has a mild psychic effect on children. But the test log makes it abundantly clear that there is a real (at least to the victim) physical effect corresponding to their perceptions. That sort of uncovered additional property would be added immediately to the description. Thing is, I can't think of a way to fix that break in Foundation characterization without disrupting the narrative flow, and it's definitely not such a big deal as to be worth that.

You mentioned up-thread that you have some author's insight that wouldn't fit into the article, any chance of getting some spoilers? I'd particularly like to know what's up with the coating. Is it a sign of something that has marked its territory, or just the accumulation of all the kids who couldn't contain themselves in the face of a mountain-sized slide?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What indeed? Never once as a child did I pluck up courage to try monkey bars. Bloody things petrified me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh God. What would the monkey bars be like?
I am honoured to put this article in the halfway mark to the big 100

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alright! It's not a huge deal either way, just annoying to me. Good job altogether

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for your feedback. I've not read 1753, but I'll give it a look.

I do have answers to a lot of the questions about how this works in my own head. I know exactly why it has these effects, and the extent of them, but I found that when I was exploring explaining them, it always worked better to leave it mysterious. Everyone has their own interpretation of it, and so on.

I'm glad my writing seems competent. I hope that perhaps next time I will use it to write up an idea you find more appealing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find this a bit too obvious and bland. The final bit was done better in SCP-1753, and there's not too much to really look at before that. Basically, I'd just like to see more done with this. I'm not sure exactly what, but that's up to you. Perhaps tribes of children lost in the heights that can escape as they get older and the way down gets shorter? Perhaps something with the effects it has on young animals and plants? Perhaps something with how it interacts simultaneously with people of different ages (like, could an adult just reach up and lift down their ten year old from the equipment and what would this look like)?

I will say that the writing is competent, and I like the obviously impossible result of the measuring tape being far longer than it really is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with you entirely, and if this were an actual report and not a piece of creative writing, I'd definitely have put conclusions in.

I did actually consider putting something at the end, and in a very early draft the line that now updates the containment procedures (exploration testing suspended) was placed at the end. I decided that it worked better near the top in the end: it felt like an important enough piece of information that it should be placed near the top of the report from an in-universe view, and for the reader it provides the first hint of 'something unexpected is going to happen'.

So, to conclude, I agree that reports should have a conclusion, but I decided to sacrifice a little bit of bureaucratic realism for the sake of storytelling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm personally against ending reports on a piece of research without any kind of final note from a researcher. It's like when you are writing an essay, you balance all facts with analysis and all facts are subsequently analyzed after you include them. Seeing it end on a test feels like a very well written essay without a conclusion. Sure it'll work, but it just feels incomplete. Even a one line closing statement would do it for me, but right now I feel like I was just left hanging (from 200 meters - pun fully intended).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly was too interested until got to the test log. That really did it for me, but It did take the test log to get there. Perhaps you could add a little something before hand to put a tad more foreshadowing on how the playground alters in size and whatnot?

E: Read it again, and got the chills when I got to the monkey bars. Imagine what those must look like to kids. Great article overall, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How is that not a perfect ending? Adding anything more to it would blunt the effect of that last line.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this a lot, but it just ends so abruptly! Please insert some kind of an ending, like even a final note from one of the doctors, or stating that testing it has been temporarily postponed. Any kind of ending!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally, I like to imagine one hardened, tattooed, scarred D-class embarrassedly pushing another on a swing, while an adenoidal scientist in a white coat goes '98… 99… Okay, that's 100 periods without anomalous incident. Let's try 500.'

And afterwards a well-meaning and simpering researcher asks them 'and how did swinging on the swing make you feel?'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
safe for use when tested by Foundation personnel.

I love the imagery this brings up: A gruff agent descending on the slide, battle-scarred hands in the air as he's like "Wheee!" While stoic doctors ride the swings, their lab coats billowing behind them…

And the rest of the stuff is good, too, like the people said. Upvoting with gusto.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The tape measure got me interested, and the final fall off the bridge won me over. +1

item.title: horrifying yet satistfying
item.content:encoded: Exactly as above mentioned title. I love this it gave me a pleasantly strange unnerving feeling of giddiness and joy reading this article, I wish you luck in your future endeavors!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like how simple the description is, leaving you with a, "Wait, that's it?" and then the test log is just brilliant. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ye had me trigger figger on the 'downvote because I was sure I'd seen this before to the dot' was waitin' fer the hook-
AND BAM. Got me straight on the jaw. Good work! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Very well done! Wasn't expecting that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice foreshadowing.

Wow, I'm so thrilled this has done well! By this time, I think all three of my first attempts had hit -10 and one may even have been deleted…

Thanks again to everyone who helped me out, especially convincing me to remove extraneous stuff I was needlessly attached to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wasn't thrilled with it until you pulled the twist. Bravo! It interested me at the end and I quite like it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a much better read now that you've cut it down to a palatable size. Nicely done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted most definitely.

I love that it leaves to the imagination how this was discovered…somewhere there are some horrified and grieving parents who have needed some heavy doses of amnestics.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the subject mental age thing. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was considering that, actually. It was in a collapsible because originally the article was a lot longer, but I quite like it, as it gives more of a sense of an appendix.

That said it does rather scream 'TWIST INCOMING' so perhaps I will remove it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, and the test log adds a lot that couldn't be said otherwise. I would recommend that you take the log out of a collapsible, however.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly at first I didn't think this would do anything for me, but after reading the test log it kind of gave me the chills inside. I also kind of like the usage of a D-Class who pretty much has the mind of a child for some reason. Have a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this when I saw it in the forums and I still like it.

item.title: Finally
item.content:encoded:
So, I've finally main-listed it.

Thanks so much to everyone who provided help and guidance; it wouldn't be where it is now without you.

I'm hoping this can be my first successful article!
